skill.name: quality-reviewer
skill.description: Reviews DevPrep AI TypeScript projects against local code quality standards using documentation, examples, and shell-based checks.
skill.summary: Run focused DevPrep AI quality checks for TypeScript code, imports, architecture, naming, file size, and complexity.
skill.author: ArieGoldkin

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, educational examples, and fixed project paths. The confirmed risk is intentional local shell execution for quality checks, so publication is acceptable with a clear warning to run it only in trusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Quality Checks
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill intentionally uses Bash scripts and local commands to inspect project files. This is legitimate for a code quality reviewer, but users should run it only in trusted repositories because it invokes project-local tooling such as ESLint and npm scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell scripts and command substitutions are clearly present and are part of the documented workflow. The commands are project-local quality checks, so the risk is environmental rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many external command and weak-crypto alerts occur inside Markdown code fences, comments, or educational TypeScript examples. These lines document quality standards and do not execute as part of the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are documentation or examples, and their surrounding text explicitly describes standards or intentional violations. I did not find evidence that these snippets are executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Project-Relative Paths Misclassified as Traversal
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Path traversal alerts point to examples of bad imports, alias guidance, hidden .claude paths, or the script locating the project root. I did not find evidence of user-controlled path traversal or arbitrary file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious paths are fixed examples or fixed repository-relative script paths. No evidence found of path input being accepted from users and dereferenced without validation.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection or Exfiltration Evidence Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious intent: Review of the available files found no prompt override text, network exfiltration, credential collection, or hidden persistence behavior. The skill purpose matches its file contents.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review and phrase search found no prompt injection indicators or network behavior. Confidence is below very high because the audit relied on reviewed files and static context, not runtime sandbox execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Quality Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill intentionally uses Bash scripts and local commands to inspect project files. This is legitimate for a code quality reviewer, but users should run it only in trusted repositories because it invokes project-local tooling such as ESLint and npm scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell scripts and command substitutions are clearly present and are part of the documented workflow. The commands are project-local quality checks, so the risk is environmental rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: DevPrep AI projects need consistent TypeScript, import, naming, and architecture standards. This skill runs local checks and provides focused guidance for fixing quality violations.
actual_capabilities: Runs scripts that check TypeScript file size, complexity, imports, architecture, and naming.
Uses ESLint and TypeScript checks through project-local commands when the full review script runs.
Explains DevPrep AI standards for six-folder architecture and path aliases.
Provides good, bad, and refactored TypeScript examples for common quality issues.
Guides fixes for oversized files, high complexity, relative imports, and interface naming.
limitations: Designed for the DevPrep AI frontend structure, not every TypeScript project.
Requires Bash and expected frontend project files to run automated checks.
Does not replace human review for product logic, security design, or accessibility.
Project-local npm scripts may behave differently across repositories.

use_cases.title: Pre-Commit Quality Gate
use_cases.description: Run the full review before committing frontend changes to catch standards violations early.

use_cases.title: Refactor Oversized Components
use_cases.description: Use the examples and standards guide to split large components into UI, hooks, types, and helpers.

use_cases.title: Review Team Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check imports, naming, folder placement, and complexity before a reviewer spends time on style issues.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this DevPrep AI change for file size, naming, imports, and architecture issues.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Violation
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain why this quality check failed and show the smallest safe fix that follows the project standards.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the quality reviewer standards to plan a refactor for this oversized component into smaller files.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full quality review, classify each violation by severity, and propose an ordered fix plan for this pull request.

output_examples.input: Review a new React component before commit.
output_examples.output: The review reports whether the file exceeds size limits, uses valid imports, follows interface naming, and stays within complexity limits.

output_examples.input: Fix a component with nested conditionals and mixed responsibilities.
output_examples.output: The response recommends early returns, extracted helper functions, and separate files for UI, hooks, types, and utilities.

output_examples.input: Check a pull request with new frontend folders.
output_examples.output: The review identifies invalid top-level folders and maps each file to the expected app, modules, shared, lib, store, or styles location.

best_practices: Run checks from the project root so relative script paths resolve correctly.
Review generated fixes before committing, especially when splitting components across files.
Use the standards document for complex violations that need architectural judgment.
anti_patterns: Do not run the scripts in untrusted repositories without reviewing project npm scripts first.
Do not treat the examples as production-ready application code.
Do not apply DevPrep AI folder rules blindly to unrelated project structures.

faq.question: What projects is this skill built for?
faq.answer: It is built for DevPrep AI TypeScript frontend projects that use the documented six-folder structure.

faq.question: Does it run code on my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. The review scripts use Bash and project-local commands, so run them only in trusted repositories.

faq.question: Can it fix every violation automatically?
faq.answer: No. It can guide fixes, but architectural refactors still need developer review.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why does it include example files with violations?
faq.answer: The bad examples demonstrate what the standards reject, while good examples show preferred patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk notice because it intentionally runs local shell quality checks.
